findings.title: ALEXA 65 "4.3K 4:3 2x Anamorphic" is a physically impossible mode — it reads out more rows / more sensor height than the A3X sensor has
findings.location: /Users/marktaylor1/Desktop/Taylorvision_Claude/Frame_Matrix/src/lib/formats.ts lines 239–252 (id "alexa65-2x-ana"): width 4320, height 3592, usedSensorWidthMm 35.64, usedSensorHeightMm 29.62; parent sensor 54.12×25.58 mm
findings.problem: The ALEXA 65 A3X sensor's full active area is 54.12 × 25.58 mm at 6560 × 3100 photosites (hard-coded correctly in the sibling "alexa65-65k-og" entry, lines 228–238). This anamorphic entry claims 3592 recorded rows and a usedSensorHeightMm of 29.62 mm. Both are impossible: 3592 > 3100 max rows, and 29.62 mm > 25.58 mm total sensor height. A programmatic sweep of every anamorphic mode in the file flags this as the ONLY one whose used-sensor height exceeds the physical sensor. It corrupts every downstream calc that reads usedSensor*: usedSensorDiagonalMm() → lens-coverage 'Covers/Vignette' verdict, computeFovDof FOV/DoF, the camera report, and any row-count file-size estimate.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace with a real ALEXA 65 anamorphic readout that stays within 3100 rows / 25.58 mm (the A3X captures anamorphic by cropping width on its native large area, not by adding rows). A defensible 2x-ana entry is ~4320×3000 (or 4096×3100) using ~35.1×24.4 mm (≤25.58 mm tall, ≤3100 rows) for a ~2.39:1 desqueezed delivery; set usedSensorHeightMm ≤ 25.58 and height ≤ 3100. Verify the exact crop against ARRI's ALEXA 65 / Codex format poster.
findings.verifierNote: Verified: A3X pitch 54.12/6560 = 8.250 µm; 3592×8.250 = 29.634 mm ≈ file's 29.62, so it literally encodes 3592 rows of a 3100-row sensor. 3592>3100 and 29.62>25.58 both true. My own sweep of all 22 modes confirms alexa65-2x-ana is the SOLE entry where usedSensorHeight>sensorHeight. A3X max 6560×3100 / 54.12×25.58 mm confirmed via vfxcamdb + CineD. Downstream consumers confirmed live: usedSensorDiagonalMm at Index.tsx:491 (lens coverage) and FovCalculator.tsx:18-19 (FOV/DoF). §B.3/§B.4 comments show this 'impossible mode' class was previously fixed elsewhere — this one slipped through.
findings.evidence: vfxcamdb.com/arri-alexa-65 and cined.com both state A3X max = 6560×3100, active area 54.12×25.58 mm, no 4:3/anamorphic mode exceeding 3100 rows. Arithmetic: 54.12/6560 = 8.250 µm; 3592 × 8.250 = 29.63 mm = the file's 29.62 figure.

findings.title: RED V-RAPTOR S35 "8K S35 4:3 2x Anamorphic" (5760×4320) is a fabricated mode — the S35 sensor's anamorphic 4:3 readout tops out at 7K (5040×3780); the in-code "Verified vs RED docs" comment is false
findings.location: /Users/marktaylor1/Desktop/Taylorvision_Claude/Frame_Matrix/src/lib/formats.ts lines 328–344 (id "raptor-s35-8k-43-2x-ana"), incl. the code comment at lines 329–331 claiming the mode is 8K 5760×4320 "Verified vs RED docs"
findings.problem: RED's own operation guide shows the V-RAPTOR Super-35 anamorphic 4:3 modes max out at 7K (5040×3780); there is NO 8K S35 4:3 mode at 5760×4320. The 5760×4320 4:3 readout exists only on the larger 8K VV sensor (correctly present at "raptor-vv-8k-43-2x-ana", lines 269–285). The in-file 'Verified vs RED docs' comment is incorrect. The arithmetic is self-consistent only because the numbers were back-fitted (5760 px × S35 pitch 3.199 µm = 18.43 mm). Result: the tool offers a recording format that does not exist on the camera, and its lens-coverage check evaluates against a fictional 18.43×13.82 mm used area.
findings.suggestedFix: Re-label as the real S35 anamorphic format: 7K S35 4:3 2x = 5040×3780, used area ≈ 16.13×12.09 mm (5040×3.199 µm, 3780×3.199 µm), OR delete it and keep only the VV 4:3 mode. Fix the misleading comment. (RED V-RAPTOR XL 8K VV Operation Guide — Super 35 Anamorphic 7K/6K: default 7K 4:3 2x = 5040×3780; no 8K S35 4:3.)
findings.verifierNote: Verified directly against RED docs (docs.red.com Super35_Ana_7K_6K page): max 4:3 mode is 7K 4:3 2x = 5040×3780; explicitly NO 8K S35 4:3 at 5760×4320 — full list fetched (7K: 5040×3780, 4536×3780, 3780×3780, 5670×3780; 6K: 5760×3240, 6144×3240). Suggested fix dims (5040×3780, 16.13×12.09 mm) match RED exactly. The 'Verified vs RED docs' comment is demonstrably false. NOTE: the height field (13.82 mm / 4320 rows) is technically within the S35 sensor (4320 ≈ 4319 max rows at 3.1995 µm pitch), so unlike Finding 1 this is a non-existent MODE, not an over-height geometric impossibility — the finding frames it correctly as fabricated.
findings.evidence: docs.red.com Super35_Ana_7K_6K guide (fetched): 7K modes use 5040×3780 (4:3 default), 6K modes 5760×3240 / 6144×3240; no 8K S35 4:3 at 5760×4320. Page title is literally 'Super 35 Anamorphic (7K/6K)'. RED VV_Ana_8K guide confirms 5760×4320 4:3-2x belongs to the VV (full-frame) sensor.

findings.title: RED V-RAPTOR VV 4:3 anamorphic note self-contradicts: calls 5760×4320 "6:5" when it is exactly 4:3
findings.location: /Users/marktaylor1/Desktop/Taylorvision_Claude/Frame_Matrix/src/lib/formats.ts line 284 (notes on id "raptor-vv-8k-43-2x-ana"): "Industry-named '4:3 ana'; actual photosite area is 6:5 (5760×4320). Crops 28.80 mm of the 40.96 mm sensor width."
findings.problem: 5760 / 4320 = 1.3333 = exactly 4:3, not 6:5 (1.20). RED documents and records this mode as 4:3 2x. The note claims the photosite area is 'actually 6:5', which is wrong and contradicts its own cited dimensions. The same 'actually 6:5' note is CORRECT on the ALEXA 35 3328×2790 entry (line 145, 1.193 ≈ 6:5) and the URSA 8192×6912 entry (line 785, 1.185 ≈ 6:5) — so the claim was copy-pasted onto a RED mode where the math doesn't hold. A DP reading the spec sheet sees a contradiction that undermines trust.
findings.suggestedFix: Correct the note to reflect that this RED VV mode genuinely is 4:3 (5760×4320), e.g. 'Full 4:3 2x anamorphic; crops 28.80 mm of the 40.96 mm sensor width (5760 px at 5.0 µm pitch).' Remove the '6:5' claim from this entry only — leave the ALEXA 35 (L145) and URSA (L785) notes as-is.
findings.verifierNote: Verified arithmetic: 5760/4320 = 1.3333 (exact 4:3); 6:5 = 1.20. Confirmed the two other '6:5' notes are correct in isolation: ALEXA35 3328/2790 = 1.1928, URSA 8192/6912 = 1.1852 — both genuinely ≈6:5. So this is a single mislabeled note from a copy-paste, exactly as the finding states. RED VV_Ana_8K labels this mode 8K 4:3 2x.
findings.evidence: Arithmetic: 5760/4320 = 1.3333 (4:3); 6:5 = 1.20. Other '6:5' notes verified correct (3328/2790 = 1.193; 8192/6912 = 1.185), isolating this as the single mislabeled note. RED VV_Ana_8K operation guide labels the mode '8K 4:3 2x' at 5760×4320.

findings.title: Two different camera modes share the identical name "Sony VENICE 2 · 5.8K 6:5 2x Anamorphic"; the duplicate (5760×4800) also does not match Sony's published 5792×4854
findings.location: /Users/marktaylor1/Desktop/Taylorvision_Claude/Frame_Matrix/src/lib/formats.ts: id "venice2-43-2x" (lines 437–450, 5792×4854) AND id "venice2-58k-65-2x" (lines 470–487, 5760×4800) — both mode:"5.8K 6:5 2x Anamorphic"
findings.problem: The VENICE 2 list contains two entries with the byte-identical mode label '5.8K 6:5 2x Anamorphic' but different pixel dimensions (5792×4854 vs 5760×4800) and different used-sensor areas. In the camera-select dropdown they are indistinguishable, so an operator cannot tell which one they are picking, and the two produce different framing charts / FDLs / file sizes. Sony's official VENICE 2 spec lists the 5.8K 6:5 anamorphic mode as 5792×4854 (24.1×20.2 mm), matching the "venice2-43-2x" entry and the BURANO entry (lines 501–514). The 5760×4800 entry does not correspond to a published Sony recording resolution; its note cites a 'CBKZ-3620A license' (the correct anamorphic license) but Sony's spec for that mode is 5792×4854, not 5760×4800 — so the entry carries an embedded factual error on top of the duplicate-name UX issue. Looks like a leftover/duplicate.
findings.suggestedFix: Remove the duplicate "venice2-58k-65-2x" (5760×4800) entry, OR if a second distinct mode is genuinely intended give it a unique, accurate name and Sony-published dimensions. Keep "venice2-43-2x" at 5792×4854 as the canonical 5.8K 6:5 2x. Optionally add Sony's real 5.8K 17:9 (5792×3056) for broader coverage.
findings.verifierNote: Confirmed both observable issues. Sony's published 5.8K 6:5 anamorphic = 5792×4854 (24.1×20.2 mm, 1–48 fps, anamorphic license) — verified via Sony format poster references and B&H/spec aggregators; matches venice2-43-2x and BURANO. The 5760×4800 entry (=exactly 1.200) is NOT a Sony-listed resolution, so it is also factually wrong, not merely a duplicate. Severity kept as ux-gap (the dominant operator-facing symptom is two indistinguishable dropdown rows) but confidence raised medium→high since Sony's number is now confirmed. Both entries verified to carry byte-identical mode strings in source.
findings.evidence: Sony VENICE2 8K Format poster (pro.sony) and B&H/spec pages: 5.8K 6:5 anamorphic = 5792×4854 (24.1×20.2 mm), 1–48 fps; 5.8K 17:9 = 5792×3056. The file's BURANO 5.8K 6:5 entry independently uses 5792×4854, corroborating it. 5760×4800 = exactly 1.200 but is not a Sony-listed resolution.

findings.title: ALEXA LF "LF 16:9 1.65x Ana (Hawk65)" attributes the 1.65x squeeze to the wrong lens system — 1.65x is Panavision Ultra Vista; Hawk65 is 1.3x
findings.location: /Users/marktaylor1/Desktop/Taylorvision_Claude/Frame_Matrix/src/lib/formats.ts lines 187–200 (id "alexa-lf-165-ana", mode "LF 16:9 1.65x Ana (Hawk65)", squeeze 1.65)
findings.problem: The mode name credits a 1.65x squeeze to 'Hawk65', but the Hawk65 (Vantage) large-format anamorphic system is a 1.3x squeeze. The 1.65x large-format squeeze is the Panavision Ultra Vista — which the app's OWN lens library lists correctly as 'Panavision Ultra Vista 1.65x' (LENSES, line 1946), while listing 'Hawk65 2x' (line 1950). So the data set contradicts itself on Hawk65's squeeze. Separately, pairing 1.65x onto a 16:9 4448×2502 capture desqueezes to 2.93:1 (4448×1.65/2502), wider than any standard delivery; Ultra Vista 1.65x is normally shot on a taller open-gate area to land ~2.39:1.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename to reference the correct lens family, e.g. 'LF 1.65x Ana (Ultra Vista)', and reconsider the capture geometry so the desqueezed result lands on a standard ratio (Ultra Vista on ALEXA LF open-gate ≈ 4448×3096 → 2.39:1, not a 16:9 sub-frame). If a Hawk65 entry is wanted, it should be 1.3x. Align the mode label with the lens library's own Ultra Vista (1.65x) / Hawk65 naming.
findings.verifierNote: Confirmed via Panavision Ultra Vista product page + FDTimes: Ultra Vista = 1.65x, Hawk65 (Vantage) = 1.3x — unambiguous. The mode name crediting 1.65x to Hawk65 is a clear brand mislabel, and the file's own LENSES array (L1946 Ultra Vista 1.65x, L1950 Hawk65 2x) self-contradicts. Verified desqueeze math: 4448×1.65/2502 = 2.933:1 (non-standard); open-gate 4448×3096 → 2.371:1 (standard ~2.39). Raised confidence medium→high: the squeeze-per-brand facts are now firmly sourced. Kept severity factual-error (the primary defect is the wrong lens-system attribution; the non-standard geometry is a secondary observation).
findings.evidence: Panavision Ultra Vista product page and FDTimes large-format coverage: Ultra Vista = 1.65x; Hawk65 (Vantage) = 1.3x. The file's LENSES array itself encodes 'Panavision Ultra Vista 1.65x' (L1946) and 'Hawk65 2x' (L1950), inconsistent with crediting 1.65x to Hawk65 on this capture mode.

findings.title: Anamorphic S35 mode '6K S35 17:9 1.3x' is missing its used-sensor crop, so FOV/DoF are computed from the FULL sensor and come out badly wrong
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:345-356 (entry id 'raptor-s35-6k-179-13x-ana'), consumed by src/components/FovCalculator.tsx:18-19 fallback `usedSensorWidthMm ?? sensorWidthMm`
findings.problem: This is the ONLY squeeze>1 entry in the whole format table that omits usedSensorWidthMm/usedSensorHeightMm (all other 21 anamorphic modes define them). The RED V-RAPTOR 8K S35 sensor is 26.21x13.82 mm across 8192x4320 px (verified vs RED docs). This 6K 17:9 1.3x mode records 6144x3240 px, i.e. it uses only 6144/8192 = 75% of sensor width and 3240/4320 = 75% of height — a physical active area of ~19.66 x 10.37 mm. Because the used-sensor fields are absent, FovCalculator falls back to the full 26.21 x 13.82 mm and then still multiplies width by the 1.3x squeeze. Result at 35mm: horizontal AOV reads 51.9 deg instead of the correct 40.1 deg (overstated ~11.8 deg) and vertical AOV reads 22.3 deg instead of 16.8 deg (overstated ~5.5 deg). Subject-plane coverage and CoC/DoF are wrong by the same proportion. Compare the sibling VV entry 'raptor-vv-8k-179-13x-ana' (formats.ts:315-327) which correctly sets usedSensorWidthMm: 40.96 because it genuinely uses the full 8192-px width — proving the pattern the S35 entry should follow.
findings.evidence: Scanned all 22 anamorphic entries: only this one lacks usedSensor dims. RED spec confirmed via web (B&H / RED docs: V-RAPTOR 8K S35 = 26.21x13.82mm, 8192x4320). Computed both cases in node: code=51.9/22.3 deg vs correct=40.1/16.8 deg at f=35mm.
findings.suggestedFix: Add usedSensorWidthMm: 19.66 and usedSensorHeightMm: 10.37 to the 'raptor-s35-6k-179-13x-ana' entry (26.21 * 6144/8192 and 13.82 * 3240/4320). General hardening: in computeFovDof / FovCalculator, when squeeze>1 but used-sensor dims are absent, this should be treated as a data error rather than silently scaling the full sensor by squeeze.
findings.verifierNote: Node-parsed all 22 squeeze>1 entries: only raptor-s35-6k-179-13x-ana lacks usedSensor dims. RED 26.21x13.82@8192x4320 web-confirmed; 6144/8192=3240/4320=0.75 -> 19.66x10.37mm. FOV math reproduced exactly (51.9/22.3 vs 40.1/16.8); type comment at formats.ts:20 + FovCalculator:18 confirm the full-sensor fallback. No test pins this entry, so bug is live.

findings.title: Circle of confusion uses the photographic 'diagonal / 1500', not the cinema-standard 'sensor width / 1500' — DoF/hyperfocal read shallower than pCam / ASC tables
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1983 `const cocMm = Math.hypot(sw, sh) / 1500;`  (UI label at src/components/FovCalculator.tsx:104)
findings.problem: The accepted digital-cinema CoC convention (ASC Manual reference tables, pCam, Cine Designer) is sensor WIDTH / 1500. This tool uses the sensor DIAGONAL / 1500, which is a photography-derived divisor applied to the diagonal — a mixed convention. For the ALEXA 35 (27.99x19.22mm) it yields CoC 0.0226 mm instead of the cinema 0.0187 mm (width/1500): ~21% larger, so hyperfocal at 35mm f/2.8 comes out 19.36 m instead of 23.48 m and total DoF reads correspondingly deeper. A focus puller or DIT cross-checking against pCam will see a consistent mismatch. (Note the diagonal value happens to sit between width/1500 and the looser 0.025 mm pCam often uses for S35, so it is not wildly off in absolute terms — but it is non-standard and, importantly, mislabelled.)
findings.evidence: Web: ASC Manual / pCam use sensor width / 1500 as the cinema norm; diagonal/1500 and diagonal/1730 (Zeiss) are the photographic forms. Computed in node: diag/1500=0.0226 vs width/1500=0.0187 (code is 0.824x the standard); hyperfocal 19.36 m vs 23.48 m.
findings.suggestedFix: Switch to the cinema convention `const cocMm = sw / 1500;` (use the used-sensor width sw, which already accounts for anamorphic crop). Then fix the UI hint on FovCalculator.tsx:104 to read 'sensor width ... / 1500' instead of 'sensor Ø ... / 1500'. If diagonal/1500 is a deliberate choice, at minimum correct the label and document that it differs from pCam.
findings.verifierNote: Line 1983 is hypot(sw,sh)/1500 (diagonal); UI label 104 literally says 'sensor Ø ... ÷ 1500'. Cinema norm = width/1500 confirmed via web (ASC/pCam; 36/1500=0.024 full-frame width example). ALEXA 35 27.99x19.22 confirmed in source; numbers reproduced exactly: 0.0226 vs 0.0187 (0.824x), hyperfocal 19.36 vs 23.48 m.

findings.title: DoF treats the T-stop input as a geometric f-number; for fast cine primes this over-states sharpness depth and there is no T-vs-f caveat
findings.location: src/components/FovCalculator.tsx:64-65 (slider labelled 'Aperture (T/f)') and src/lib/formats.ts:1984-1987 (DoF uses fNumber directly)
findings.problem: The aperture slider is labelled 'T/f' and feeds straight into the hyperfocal/DoF math as the geometric f-number N. Depth of field is governed by the physical (f-number) aperture, not the T-stop, and on fast cine glass the two diverge (a T2.8 lens often sits around f/2.5 due to transmission losses). A working pro entering the marked T-stop off the lens barrel will get slightly optimistic DoF. The combined 'T/f' label invites exactly this conflation without flagging it.
findings.evidence: Standard optics: DoF formulas use the geometric f-number c; T-stop is a transmission-corrected exposure number, typically 1/8 to 1/2 stop slower than the f-number on cine primes. Code passes fstop -> fNumber with no adjustment or note.
findings.suggestedFix: Either label the slider 'f-number' and add a one-line note that DoF assumes geometric f (enter the lens's f-number, ~1/3-stop faster than its T-stop), or keep 'T-stop' and apply/disclose that DoF is computed treating T as f. Even a small hint line under the DoF panel would close the gap for users reading T-stops off the barrel.
findings.verifierNote: Slider label 'Aperture (T/f)' at FovCalculator:64; fstop passed directly as fNumber at line 27 with no transmission correction. DoF physics governed by geometric f-number, T>=f (web-confirmed) -> entering barrel T-stop yields optimistically deep DoF. Correctly scoped as medium ux-gap; no caveat present in UI.
findings.domain: Optics

findings.title: Super-35 equivalence reference is annotated '3-perf' but the dimensions used (24.89 x 18.66 mm) are 4-perf / full-aperture S35
findings.location: src/components/FovCalculator.tsx:9 `const S35_DIAG = Math.hypot(24.89, 18.66); // ~31.1 mm — Super 35 (3-perf)`
findings.problem: 24.89 x 18.66 mm is a 4:3 frame (diagonal 31.11 mm) — that is 4-perf / full-aperture Super-35, the classic 'maximum S35' reference. 3-perf Super-35 is ~24.89 x 13.86 mm (close to 16:9, diagonal ~28.5 mm). The chosen dimensions are a legitimate 'full S35' equivalence reference, but the '(3-perf)' comment is simply the wrong label and will mislead anyone reading the source or reasoning about which S35 the crop factor is relative to.
findings.evidence: Computed: hypot(24.89,18.66)=31.11mm (4:3, full/4-perf S35); hypot(24.89,13.86)=28.49mm (3-perf, ~16:9). The two S35 perf formats have materially different diagonals.
findings.suggestedFix: Change the comment to '(4-perf / full-aperture S35)', or if 3-perf is intended, change the dimensions to 24.89 x 13.86 (and re-derive the ~28.5 mm diagonal). The equivalence value itself is defensible either way once the label matches the numbers.
findings.verifierNote: Source line 9 confirmed verbatim with '(3-perf)' comment. Reproduced: 24.89x18.66=31.11mm ratio 1.334 (~4:3, 4-perf/full S35); 3-perf=24.89x13.86 (~16:9, 28.49mm) web-confirmed (Wikipedia/Grokipedia: 4-perf 24.89x18.66, 3-perf 24.89x13.9). Dimensions valid as 'full S35'; only the annotation is wrong. factual-error (source-comment) accurate.

findings.title: Anamorphic circle of confusion is computed on the squeezed photosite area, not the desqueezed delivery frame, so DoF is marginally optimistic for high-squeeze modes
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1983 (cocMm) in combination with the anamorphic used-sensor dims
findings.problem: For anamorphic modes, sw/sh are the physical (squeezed) photosite dimensions (e.g. ALEXA 35 6:5 2x = 20.22 x 16.94 mm). The audience sees the DESQUEEZED frame, which is ~2x wider, so a given blur circle on the sensor is magnified more horizontally on the final image than the squeezed-area CoC implies. A stricter model would base CoC on the delivered (desqueezed) frame, giving slightly shallower acceptable DoF on the long horizontal axis. This is a refinement, not a correctness break — most pocket calculators also ignore it — but a high-end anamorphic show would notice DoF that errs optimistic.
findings.evidence: Anamorphic literature (focal/squeeze model, verified via web): 2x squeeze captures the horizontal FOV of a half-focal spherical and is stretched in post; horizontal magnification of the desqueeze is the squeeze factor, which is what scales perceived blur on the wide axis.
findings.suggestedFix: Optionally compute CoC from the desqueezed frame width (sw*sq) for the width-based cinema convention — i.e. CoC = (sw*sq)/1500 keeps it consistent with the delivered image. At minimum, note in the UI that DoF is computed on the captured (squeezed) sensor and is a touch optimistic for high-squeeze anamorphic.
findings.verifierNote: CoC at line 1983 uses squeezed used-sensor dims (ALEXA 35 6:5 2x = 20.22x16.94 confirmed at formats.ts:140-141); sq never enters the CoC. Premise is physically sound (desqueeze magnifies horizontal blur by squeeze factor) and honestly scoped as optional/low. Caveat: suggested fix (sw*sq)/1500 only fully coheres once Finding 2's width-based CoC is adopted; as a standalone enhancement it is valid and low-impact.

findings.title: ARRIRAW HDE storage is understated ~2.7x — wrong compression ratio (3.5–4x vs real ~1.67x)
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1247-1257 (codec id 'arriraw-hde': bppx 3.0, rateLabel '~1.05 Gbps... ~470 GB/hr', notes '~3.5–4× compression')
findings.problem: The code models ARRIRAW HDE as ~3.5–4x compression (bppx 3.0 = one quarter of uncompressed 12bpp), giving ~1.05 Gbps / ~470 GB/hr. ARRI/Codex officially state HDE is LOSSLESS and produces files ~60% of original size (~1.67:1, ~40% saving), NOT 3.5–4x. A DIT planning media counts off this tool buys ~2.5–2.7x too few mags and runs out of media on set.
findings.evidence: Verified at arri.com 'High Density Encoding (HDE)' and help.codex.online: 'ARRIRAW images encoded with HDE are approximately 60% of the original size' / 'reduces file size by over 40% ... bit-exact match when decoded'. Newsshooter ALEXA 35 review independently: HDE clip is '50 to 60% of its original size'. Code bppx 3.0 implies 4x (25% of original), contradicting ARRI by ~2.4x. Code math verified: 3.0×4608×3164×24/1e6 = 1050 Mbps = 472 GB/hr (matches finding).
findings.suggestedFix: Set HDE to ~60% of uncompressed ARRIRAW. The exact bppx tracks whatever uncompressed bppx is chosen: at ~60% of ~12.74 (corrected uncompressed, see ARRIRAW finding) HDE bppx ≈ 7.6; at 60% of 13.27 it is ~7.96. Update rateLabel to ~2.7–2.8 Gbps @ 4.6K 24p / ~1.2 TB/hr and notes to 'Bit-exact lossless, ~40% smaller than uncompressed ARRIRAW (~1.7:1)'.
findings.verifierNote: Read formats.ts:1247-1257; code math reproduced (1050 Mbps/472 GB-hr). ARRI/Codex 'approx 60% of original / >40% reduction, bit-exact' and newsshooter '50-60%' confirm HDE is ~1.67:1 not 3.5–4x. Only the suggested HDE bppx number depends on the uncompressed value (7.6 vs 7.96); direction and ~2.5x understatement are solid.
findings.domain: Storage & Data — codec data-rate library, ProRes rate-table lookup, and media-plan card math (src/lib/formats.ts, src/components/FileSizeCalculator.tsx)

findings.title: REDCODE HQ/MQ/LQ data rates ~30–38% too low — calibration target (2.25 Gbps @ 8K HQ) is wrong
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1307-1332 (red-r3d-hq bppx 2.6, red-r3d-mq bppx 1.85, red-r3d-lq bppx 1.3) and comment 'calibrated so 8K 17:9 24p HQ ≈ 2.25 Gbps'
findings.problem: Code's bppx yields HQ 2206 Mbps (276 MB/s), MQ 1570 Mbps (196 MB/s), LQ 1103 Mbps (138 MB/s) at 8K 17:9 24p. RED's published 8K 17:9 23.98 rates are far higher, so every REDCODE estimate (V-RAPTOR, Monstro, Komodo, DXL paths) under-reports card counts and offload time. The stated anchor 'HQ ≈ 2.25 Gbps' is itself wrong; RED HQ is ~3.4–3.57 Gbps.

findings.suggestedFix: Recalibrate to RED's published figures (HQ bppx ≈ 4.2, MQ ≈ 2.95, LQ ≈ 1.85 for the V1.3 set; ≈4.0/2.8/1.75 if matching the V1.7 set). findings.title: Entire ProRes rate table is shifted one frame-rate notch — ~20% too high at 24/25fps
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1202-1235 (PRORES_*_TABLE '24'/'25' columns) and rateLabels at 1264-1304 ('500 Mbps @ HD24', '220 Mbps @ HD24', etc.)

True Apple 24/25p figures are ~20% lower (422HQ 1080p25 = 184, 4444 1080p25 = 275), so at the dominant 23.976/24/25 acquisition rates every ProRes size and the comparison table reads ~20% high, and the '30' column reads ~25% high vs Apple's 29.97 figure.

Code table 422HQ '1920' has {24:220,25:229,30:275}; 4444 has {24:330,25:344,30:413} — i.e. the 29.97 headline sits in the 24 key.

findings.title: ARRIRAW uncompressed ~6% low and bit depth mislabeled (ALEXA 35 is 13-bit, not 12-bit)
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1158-1161 (comment 'ARRIRAW uncompressed is 12 bits per pixel ⇒ bppx = 12.0') and 1239-1246 (arriraw-og bppx 12, rateLabel '~4.2 Gbps @ 4.6K 24p · ~1.85 TB/hr')
findings.problem: The ALEXA 35 records 13-bit log ARRIRAW (code labels it 12-bit, bppx 12.0). The code under-reports the flagship RAW codec on the most common ARRI job. Direction of the finding is correct, but the magnitude and the suggested bppx in the original finding are off because they conflate frame rates.
findings.evidence: ARRIRAW bit depth: newsshooter ALEXA 35 review lists 'ARRIRAW 13 bit log' (vs 12-bit on older ALEXA LF) — confirmed. Data rate: ARRI's published 4644 Mbit/s for 4.6K 3:2 OG (4608×3164) is quoted at 25 fps per multiple secondary sources (vahire / cameradatacalculator: 4644 @25fps; ~4458 Mbps / 2.01 TB-hr @24fps). Implied bppx = 4644e6/(4608·3164·25) = 12.74 (equivalently 4458e6 @24fps). Code bppx 12 → @25fps 4374 Mbps vs 4644 = 5.8% low; @24fps 4199 vs 4458 = 5.8% low. The finding's '~10% low' and bppx 13.27 came from treating 4644 as a 24fps figure (4644e6/(4608·3164·24)=13.27), which double-counts the fps gap.
findings.suggestedFix: Set arriraw-og bppx ≈ 12.74 (matches ARRI 4644 Mbps @25fps and ~4458 @24fps) and relabel as 13-bit: '13-bit · ~4.46 Gbps @ 4.6K 3:2 24p (4.64 Gbps @25p) · ~2.0 TB/hr'. Update the 1158-1161 comment to note ALEXA 35 ARRIRAW is 13-bit (older 12-bit ALEXAs ≈ bppx 12). If a primary ARRI source confirms 4644 is a 24fps figure, use bppx 13.27 instead — but the weight of sources says 25fps.
findings.verifierNote: Read 1158-1161 and 1239-1246; code math reproduced (4199 @24, 4374 @25). 13-bit label fix confirmed (newsshooter). But '~10% low / bppx 13.27' is wrong: ARRI's 4644 is the 25fps rate (vahire, cameradatacalculator), so matched-fps understatement is ~5.8% and the right bppx is ~12.74. findings.title: lookupProResRate assumes a 16:9 reference frame, so DCI 4K/8K table rows undershoot their own published rate by ~6%
findings.location: src/lib/formats.ts:1780 (refPx = bestW * (bestW * 9 / 16)) inside lookupProResRate, applied to the '4096' and '8192' rows
findings.problem: The linear rescale derives reference pixels as width×(width×9/16), assuming every row is 16:9. The '4096' rows hold DCI-4K rates (4096×2160, ~1.90:1) and '8192' rows hold 8K-DCI (8192×4320). A native-DCI request gives askPx/refPx = (4096×2160)/(4096×2304) = 0.9375, returning 707 Mbps instead of the table's 754 (422HQ DCI 4K) and 2827 instead of 3015 at 8K — ~6% low on DCI deliverables, stacking on the fps-axis error.
findings.evidence: Code line 1780 forces height = width×9/16 = 2304 (4096 row) and 4608 (8192 row), but DCI 4K is 2160 tall and 8K DCI 4320. Reproduced exactly: lookup(422HQ, 4096×2160, 24) = 706.9 (table 754); lookup(422HQ, 8192×4320, 24) = 2826.6 (table 3015). HD/UHD rows are truly 16:9 so unaffected.
findings.suggestedFix: Store each row's actual reference height (or full reference resolution) instead of assuming 9/16: 4096 row → 2160, 8192 row → 4320, HD/UHD keep 9/16. Then a native-resolution request returns the published rate exactly.
findings.verifierNote: Read lookupProResRate (1756-1785). Reproduced the bug numerically: native DCI-4K 422HQ returns 706.9 vs published 754 (0.9375×), 8K 2826.6 vs 3015. Pure self-contained logic error, no external spec needed. Confirmed.

findings.title: Media-plan 'fill %' hint exceeds 100% when a camera needs more than one card per day
findings.location: src/components/FileSizeCalculator.tsx:397 (utilisation = (perCamGB / cardGB) * 100) and 411 (hint '... · {utilisation}% fill')
findings.problem: Utilisation is perCamGB/cardGB even though cardsPerCamPerDay (line 392) already ceils to multiple cards. When a camera shoots more than one card's worth in a day (routine for RAW, e.g. 1.5 TB/day on a 1 TB card) the chip reads '150% fill', which is nonsense to the AC/DIT. Intended meaning (avg fill across cards actually used) is perCamGB/(cards×cardGB) = 75%.
findings.evidence: Code: line 392 cardsPerCamPerDay = max(1, ceil(perCamGB/cardGB)); line 397 utilisation ignores the ceil. Reproduced: perCamGB=1500, cardGB=1000 → 2 cards, hint shows '150% fill'; true average fill 1500/(2×1000) = 75%.
findings.suggestedFix: Compute fill against cards actually allocated: utilisation = perCamGB / (cardsPerCamPerDay × cardGB) × 100, optionally annotating the last card's partial fill. Caps at ≤100% and reads correctly for multi-card days.
findings.verifierNote: Read FileSizeCalculator.tsx:391-411. Reproduced: 1500 GB on 1000 GB card → 2 cards but hint '150% fill'; correct average is 75%. Self-contained UX/logic bug, confirmed.

findings.title: ACEScc is named in the UI label ('what most colorists pick over ACEScc') but never defined — minor for a pro audience expecting the toe/no-toe distinction
findings.location: src/lib/aces.ts:196-199 (GRADE.use) and AcesPanel.tsx:70
findings.problem: The grade working-space hint reads 'Grade — log-like toe; what most colorists pick over ACEScc.' ACEScc is referenced as the thing-not-chosen but is never explained anywhere in the panel. The actual technical distinction a colourist cares about is that ACEScct adds a linear segment (toe) at the bottom of the curve for more natural lift/shadow behaviour on a trackball, whereas ACEScc is a pure log curve with no toe (steeper shadow response). The current copy implies ACEScct simply 'has a toe' without contrasting it to ACEScc's lack of one, so the parenthetical name-drop adds little.
findings.suggestedFix: Either drop the 'over ACEScc' clause, or make the distinction explicit, e.g. 'Grade — log curve with a linear toe (ACEScc has no toe → harsher shadow response); the usual colourist pick.' This is the one detail a senior colourist would expect stated precisely.
findings.verifierNote: Verified source text at aces.ts:198 and the render at AcesPanel.tsx:70 — quotes match. Web-confirmed the underlying claim (ACEScct = log + linear toe near black; ACEScc = pure log, straight line on a log/log plot, harsher shadow lift). Existing copy is not factually wrong, just less explicit than a pro would want — correctly an editorial enhancement, not a bug.
findings.evidence: ACES spec (docs.acescentral.com): ACEScct = ACEScc log with an added linear segment ('toe') below ~0.0078 linear; ACEScc is pure log. The toe is why colourists prefer ACEScct for trackball grading.

findings.title: Interchange hint calls ACES2065-1 'the only ACES handoff/archival encoding (OpenEXR)' — conflates the encoding with its container
findings.location: src/lib/aces.ts:204-207 (INTERCHANGE.use)
findings.problem: The hint reads 'Interchange & archival — the only ACES handoff/archival encoding (OpenEXR).' Two small imprecisions for a pro: (1) ACES2065-1 (AP0, linear) is indeed the designated interchange/archival encoding, but OpenEXR is the *container/file format*, not the encoding — and OpenEXR can equally hold ACEScg or other data; conversely the SMPTE ST 2065-4 ACES Container is the formal archival file spec. (2) Saying it is 'the only' archival encoding is fine for interchange, but the parenthetical '(OpenEXR)' may mislead a junior op into thinking any OpenEXR = ACES2065-1.
findings.suggestedFix: Reword to separate encoding from container, e.g. 'Interchange & archival — the designated ACES handoff/archival encoding (linear AP0). Carried as OpenEXR / SMPTE ST 2065-4 ACES container; tag it AP0, since EXR alone doesn't guarantee the encoding.'
findings.verifierNote: Source text at aces.ts:206 matches the quote. Web-confirmed the standards split: ST 2065-1 defines the ACES2065-1 encoding (AP0/linear); ST 2065-4 defines the OpenEXR-based ACES Container file with mandatory metadata; OpenEXR is a general HDR container that does not imply ACES2065-1. Encoding-vs-container conflation is real; severity correctly minor (a pro reads '(OpenEXR)' charitably as 'carried in EXR'), so 'low' confidence / enhancement is apt.
findings.evidence: SMPTE ST 2065-1 defines ACES2065-1 (AP0 primaries, linear). The ACES container is SMPTE ST 2065-4 (an OpenEXR-based file). OpenEXR is a general HDR container that can hold ACEScg/ACES2065-1/arbitrary data — the format does not imply the encoding.

findings.title: Parallel edges between the same two nodes draw and label on top of each other (analysis/trim collide)
findings.location: src/components/MasteringWorkflow.tsx:288-304 (SVG paths) and :308-331 (edge chips); data in src/lib/mastering.ts:166-180 hdrDeriveEdges
findings.problem: Edge geometry is derived purely from the two endpoint node positions, with no offset for parallel edges. Several node pairs have MORE THAN ONE edge between them: hdrHero→dvxml has both an 'analyze' (L1) and a 'trim' edge (mastering.ts:168-169), and the same duplication exists in buildCustomGraph (mastering.ts:343-344). Because x1,y1,x2,y2 and dx are identical for both, the two Bézier paths are drawn exactly on top of each other (only one is visible — and they share the same colour/dash since both are non-ACES grey-dotted). Worse, the edge chips are positioned at a single computed midpoint mx=(a.x+COL_W+b.x)/2, my=(a.y+b.y)/2+NODE_H/2 (lines 311-312), so the 'L1' and 'trim' chips render at the identical coordinate and overlap into an unreadable stack. A colourist reading the graph cannot see that two distinct operations (Dolby Vision L1 analysis vs. the TID1 trim) connect those nodes.
findings.evidence: hdrDeriveEdges returns two edges with the same from/to ('hdrHero'→'dvxml'): {op:'analyze'} and {op:'trim'} (mastering.ts:168-169). In the renderer both the <path> key={i} (line 300) and the chip <div> key={i} (line 316) compute position only from pos.get(e.from)/pos.get(e.to) with no per-edge fan-out, so duplicate (from,to) pairs collapse to one visible line and one chip location.
findings.suggestedFix: Group edges by (from,to), compute an index within each group, and apply a perpendicular offset to both the Bézier control points and the chip midpoint (e.g. spread chips vertically by ±14px * (idx - (n-1)/2)). Alternatively merge the analyze+trim pair into a single edge whose label lists both operations.
findings.verifierNote: Read both code sites: the two hdrHero→dvxml edges (mastering.ts:168-169 and dup at :343-344) have identical endpoints; renderer (MasteringWorkflow.tsx:291-292,311-312) uses no per-edge offset; both edges are acesManaged:false + dotted, so stroke #64748b and dash '2 5' are identical — paths and chips genuinely coincide. Confirmed. Minor: in the DEFAULT compact state the later (trim) chip simply paints over the L1 chip (one hidden, not a jumbled pile); the 'unreadable stack' is most acute when a node is selected and both full labels expand at the same midpoint (max-w-[150px] multi-line). Core defect holds; severity logic-bug appropriate.

findings.title: Dolby Vision trim label '...100 nit (first), +600 nit' conflates the SDR derive with an unrelated PQ trim target
findings.location: src/lib/mastering.ts:169 (and identical at :344) — edge hdrHero→dvxml: "TID1 trim → SDR Rec.709 100 nit (first), +600 nit"
findings.problem: The label tacks '+600 nit' onto the edge that establishes the TID1 = SDR Rec.709 100-nit trim. In the Dolby Vision trim ladder, TID1 (SDR Rec.709 / 100-nit) is the mandatory foundation trim that must be done first — that part is correct. But a '600-nit' target is a *separate* additional trim target (e.g. a ~600-nit P3 consumer OLED), not a step in producing the SDR Rec.709 100-nit derive. Putting '+600 nit' on the heroId→dvxml→SDR path implies a 600-nit pass is part of the SDR-down workflow, which misrepresents the trim model. The common documented targets are Rec.709 100-nit (required first), P3 48-nit, and PQ 108-nit; 600-nit is an optional extra target typically added for 2000/4000-nit masters, not an SDR step.
findings.evidence: Dolby/Netflix guidance: the TID1 (SDR Rec.709/100-nit) trim pass must be completed before any others; common targets are '709 100nits, P3 48nits, and PQ 108nits'; 600-nit is described as an optional additional target (e.g. 600-nit P3 OLED), added for high-nit masters, not an SDR step (Dolby Vision Color Grading Best Practices v4.x; Netflix Dolby Vision Metadata Overview; mixinglight DV part 2).
findings.suggestedFix: Drop the dangling '+600 nit' or move it to its own optional edge. A correct label would be: 'TID1 trim → SDR Rec.709 100-nit (required first; foundation for interpolation)'. If you want to show extra targets, add a separate dotted edge such as 'Optional additional trims: P3 48-nit, PQ 108-nit, ~600-nit P3 OLED' rather than appending to the SDR line.
findings.verifierNote: Web-verified (Dolby BPG v4.x, Netflix Partner Help, mixinglight): TID1 SDR Rec.709 100-nit is the required-first foundation trim (so '(first)' is correct), and 600-nit is an OPTIONAL additional target added for high-nit (2000/4000) masters, explicitly NOT part of the SDR-100 derivation ('in addition to your earlier trims'). Appending '+600 nit' to the SDR-derive edge does conflate two distinct things. Finding correct; factual-error severity justified. One refinement: the 600-nit is most commonly cited as a P3 OLED target rather than strictly 'PQ 600', but this does not change the verdict.

findings.title: SDR IMF labelled 'App 2' on deprecated ST 2067-20 — current practice is a single App 2E carrying SDR+HDR
findings.location: src/lib/mastering.ts:155 — imfsdr node: label 'IMF App 2 SDR / Broadcast HD', container 'ST 2067-20'
findings.problem: The graph ships two separate IMF deliverables: an HDR 'App 2E (DV)' (ST 2067-21) and an SDR 'App 2' (ST 2067-20). SMPTE's 2020 revision consolidated App 2 into App 2E: ST 2067-21 (App 2E) now incorporates the provisions of ST 2067-20 (App 2) and App 2 is deprecated for App 2E. In real Netflix/studio delivery, SDR and HDR are typically carried within the App 2E framework, not as a distinct legacy App 2 ST 2067-20 package. Presenting App 2 (ST 2067-20) as a current, separate deliverable can mislead planning. (The ST number itself is historically correct for App 2, so this is a currency/clarity issue rather than a wrong number.)
findings.evidence: SMPTE: 'ST 2067-21 IMF Application #2E has been revised so that it incorporates the provisions of both ST 2067-20 and ST 2067-21 ... ST 2067-20 (Application #2) is now deprecated for Application #2E' (SMPTE/SportsVideo June 2020; SMPTE ST 2067 Wikipedia).
findings.suggestedFix: Either relabel as 'IMF App 2E — SDR Rec.709 (ST 2067-21)' to reflect the consolidated standard, or add a note that App 2 (ST 2067-20) is deprecated and the SDR variant is normally delivered within the App 2E package. If keeping App 2 deliberately to show a legacy path, annotate it as legacy.
findings.verifierNote: Web-verified against SMPTE 2020 revision reporting: ST 2067-20 (App 2) is deprecated for App 2E, and ST 2067-21 now incorporates both. findings.title: CSV/PDF/on-screen 'End' date is the exclusive end (Monday AFTER the phase), one day past the real finish
findings.location: src/lib/scheduleExport.ts:63 (buildCSV) and :189 (renderGanttCanvas label); src/components/PostSchedule.tsx:526,571 (on-screen end label)
findings.problem: A phase's end is computed as dateAtISO(anchor, b.start + b.dur) and presented in a column literally headed 'End' (CSV line 59) and in the bar label as 'start–end'. That value is the EXCLUSIVE end — i.e. the Monday that starts the NEXT week. For a 1-week Grade starting Mon 01 Jun, the tool shows 'End: 22 Jun' for a 3-week grade, but the grade's last working day is Fri 19/Sun 21 Jun; the 22nd is when the next phase begins. The ICS export deliberately and CORRECTLY uses this same value because RFC 5545 DTEND with VALUE=DATE is non-inclusive (verified against RFC 5545 §3.8.2.2). The bug is that the SAME exclusive number is surfaced to humans as 'End'/'Finish', which every PM tool (MS Project, Smartsheet) treats as the LAST inclusive working day. A post supervisor reading the CSV/PDF will book the suite/colourist one day too long or misread the wrap date by a day on every single phase.
findings.suggestedFix: Keep the ICS DTEND exactly as-is (it is correct). For the human-facing End in CSV, the PDF/PNG bar label, and the on-screen tooltip, subtract one day: display fmtLong(dateAtISO(anchor, b.start + b.dur) - 1 day) i.e. addDaysISO(dateAtISO(anchor, b.start+b.dur), -1). That makes 'End' the inclusive last day (Sun 21 Jun for the 3-week grade). Alternatively rename the column to 'Ends before' and add a note, but the inclusive date is what a producer expects.
findings.verifierNote: Reproduced in Node: anchor 2026-06-01 (Mon), 3-wk phase wk0 -> CSV End=dateAtISO(anchor,3)=2026-06-22 (week-4 Mon), real last day 2026-06-21; on-screen endD (line 526, Math.max(dur,1)) yields identical 22 Jun; ICS DTEND=20260622. Web-verified RFC 5545 §3.8.2.2/§3.6.1: DTEND VALUE=DATE is non-inclusive (canonical Jun28–30 inclusive -> DTEND Jul01). CSV header literally 'End' (line 59). All claims hold.

findings.title: 'JSON — backup' export has no matching import — a backup you can never restore
findings.location: src/components/PostSchedule.tsx:59 (EXPORTS label 'JSON — backup'); src/lib/scheduleExport.ts:54-56,201 (buildJSON / case 'json')
findings.problem: The export menu offers 'JSON — backup', strongly implying you can save the schedule to a file and reload it later (the universal meaning of 'backup'). But there is NO file-import path anywhere in the component: loadBars only reads localStorage KEY_BARS, and the only restore mechanism is the in-app Versions list (also localStorage-only). grep confirms buildJSON is the sole JSON touch-point and there is no FileReader / input[type=file] / accept= handler. So if the user clears the browser, switches machines, or the localStorage is evicted, the downloaded JSON is dead weight — there is no way to load it back. For a freelancer carrying a schedule between a prep laptop and an edit suite, this is a real data-loss trap dressed up as a safety net.

findings.title: Duration editor caps at 104 weeks but the canvas/timeline hard-caps at 80, silently hiding and not exporting the tail of long phases
findings.location: src/components/PostSchedule.tsx:140 (weeksToShow = Math.min(..., 80)) and :613-616 (duration input max={104}); src/lib/scheduleExport.ts:103,124,136 (renderGanttCanvas uses ctx.weeksToShow)
findings.problem: The duration number field accepts up to 104 weeks (and drag-resize 'r' has NO upper clamp at all — line 181 is just Math.max(1, d0+dw)). But weeksToShow is clamped to a maximum of 80. So a 104-week phase from week 0 renders only its first 80 weeks; the bar visually runs off the right edge and the last ~24 weeks are clipped. Critically, the PNG/PDF export builds its canvas width from the same capped weeksToShow (W = LBL + weeksToShow*WW), so the exported Gantt is truncated too — the delivered PDF silently drops the back end of any long-running track (e.g. a 13-week VFX pushed late, or a feature with a 2-year post). The user gets a chart that looks complete but isn't.
findings.suggestedFix: Make weeksToShow consistent with the data: raise the cap to at least cover max bar end (e.g. Math.min(Math.max(Math.ceil(maxEnd)+6, 24), 120)) or remove the hard 80 cap for export specifically so renderGanttCanvas always spans every bar. Also add an upper clamp to the resize-right handler (mirror the editor's 104) so drag can't create an off-canvas bar.
findings.verifierNote: Reproduced: weeksToShow=Math.min(Math.max(110,24),80)=80 for maxEnd=104 -> 24 weeks clipped. renderGanttCanvas (scheduleExport.ts:103) W=LBL+weeksToShow*WW and loops i<weeksToShow (:124,:136), so export truncates identically. Confirmed line 181 resize-right = Math.max(1, it.d0+dw) with no upper bound; duration input max={104} at :613/:615. All claims hold.

findings.title: Loading a saved/imported version does no per-bar validation, unlike loadBars — a corrupt version can break drag/keys
findings.location: src/components/PostSchedule.tsx:47-52 (loadVersions) and :342-345 (loadVersion); compare with :105-115 (loadBars which filters)
findings.problem: loadBars() defensively filters each bar (requires name:string, finite start, finite dur) before seeding state. But loadVersions() only checks Array.isArray(v.bars), and loadVersion() does setBars(v.bars.map(b => ({...b}))) with NO per-bar filtering. So a version whose bars lost a field — via a future schema change, a hand-edited localStorage, or a partial write — loads straight into state. A bar missing 'id' becomes React key=undefined (key collisions, mis-rendered rows) and breaks every drag handler, which finds bars by id (e.g. onDown line 211 does bars.find(x=>x.id===gid)! and would throw on the non-null assertion). loadBars itself also doesn't require id/color, so a stored bar without an id passes its filter too (confirmed: id and color come back undefined).
findings.suggestedFix: Run version bars through the same sanitiser used by loadBars, and additionally backfill id (uid()) and color (PALETTE fallback) for any bar missing them, in BOTH loadBars and loadVersion. e.g. map bars to { id: b.id || uid(), color: b.color || PALETTE[i%len], name: String(b.name), start: +b.start||0, dur: Math.max(0,+b.dur||0), ... } after the finite-number filter.
findings.verifierNote: Verified: loadBars filter (line 111) on {name:'x',start:0,dur:2} returns true with id=undefined,color=undefined. loadVersion (line 343) is setBars(v.bars.map(b=>({...b}))) — no filter. loadVersions (line 50) only checks v && Array.isArray(v.bars). onDown line 211 uses bars.find(x=>x.id===gid)! non-null assertion. Medium severity correct: app's own saveVersion always writes uid()/PALETTE bars, so trigger needs external corruption. Robustness gap, all claims hold.

findings.title: Template/new-phase 'current week' uses Math.round, so it can land a week late when today is past mid-week
findings.location: src/components/PostSchedule.tsx:319 (newStart), :330 (applyTemplate base), :331
findings.problem: applyTemplate's tooltip and empty-state copy promise the template is laid down 'starting from the current week', and newStart() positions new phases/keyframes at the current week. Both use Math.round(todayWeeks). Because todayWeeks is a fractional week offset from the Monday anchor, the rounding can push the start to the NEXT week. Specifically, FRIDAY, SATURDAY and SUNDAY (offset 4/7≈0.571, 5/7≈0.714, 6/7≈0.857) round UP to the next week index — NOT Thursday as the original finding claimed (Thursday=3/7≈0.429 rounds DOWN to 0, staying in the current week). So adding a phase on a Friday/weekend, or applying the template then, silently starts it the following Monday rather than 'this week'. Monday through Thursday are unaffected. A scheduler expecting work to begin in the current week sees it pushed back seven days.
findings.suggestedFix: Use Math.floor(todayWeeks) instead of Math.round for newStart (line 319) and the applyTemplate base (line 330), so 'current week' always means the Monday of the week containing today, regardless of weekday.
findings.verifierNote: Core bug confirmed (Math.round at :319 and :330; UI promises 'current week' at :329/:400/:479; fix Math.floor correct). CORRECTED the trigger: enumerated weekday offsets in Node — Mon–Thu (0..0.429) round to 0 (current week); only Fri/Sat/Sun (0.571/0.714/0.857) round to 1. The finding's 'any day from Thursday onward' and its Thursday example are factually WRONG; the real threshold is Friday-onward. Severity (enhancement) unchanged.

findings.title: Dolby Vision Profile 5 is 10-bit, not 12-bit
findings.location: src/lib/glossary-data.json:2547 (term "Dolby Vision Profiles", line 2540)
findings.problem: The definition states "Profile 5 is single-layer 12-bit (Apple TV+, legacy UHD Blu-ray)." Dolby Vision Profile 5 is a single-layer 10-bit HEVC stream in the proprietary IPT-PQ-c2 colourspace. The 12-bit figure describes the studio master, not the delivered Profile 5 stream, whose whole design goal is to carry 12-bit-derived imagery inside a 10-bit stream that consumer HEVC decoders can handle.
findings.evidence: Verified at line 2547: definition literally reads "Profile 5 is single-layer 12-bit". Dolby professional support article states "The output of profile 5 is effectively 10 bit" and describes Profile 5 as single-layer in proprietary IPT-PQ-c2. Wikipedia Dolby Vision profiles table lists Profile 5 as 10-bit HEVC, single-layer (no enhancement layer), IPTPQc2; the 12-bit stream is produced only by the dual-layer Profile 7 (BL+EL).
findings.suggestedFix: Change to: "Profile 5 is single-layer 10-bit in Dolby's proprietary IPT-PQ-c2 colourspace (Apple TV+, legacy UHD Blu-ray) — not backward-compatible with HDR10." Optionally note the source master is 12-bit. (Aside: Profile 5 is not the UHD Blu-ray profile — that is dual-layer Profile 7 — but the original entry also pairs it with Blu-ray, so this fix preserves the entry's existing parenthetical.)
findings.verifierNote: Read line 2547 verbatim; cross-checked Dolby support article ("output of profile 5 is effectively 10 bit") and Wikipedia profiles table (Profile 5 = 10-bit single-layer IPTPQc2). 12-bit applies to the master / Profile 7, not the Profile 5 stream — finding is correct.

findings.title: Dolby Vision Profile 8 mislabelled 'dual-layer-compatible' when it is single-layer
findings.location: src/lib/glossary-data.json:2547 (term "Dolby Vision Profiles", line 2540)
findings.problem: The definition calls Profile 8.1 and 8.4 "both dual-layer-compatible streaming profiles". Profile 8 is by definition a single-layer system: one base layer (HDR10 for 8.1, HLG for 8.4) plus an RPU metadata sidecar carrying the dynamic trim. The RPU is metadata, not a second video (enhancement) layer. The professionally meaningful distinction is that Profile 8 is single-layer whereas Profile 7 (UHD Blu-ray) is dual-layer (base + FEL/MEL). Calling 8.x 'dual-layer' inverts the key fact.
findings.evidence: Verified at line 2547: text reads "(both dual-layer-compatible streaming profiles)". Dolby professional support: "Profile 8 is a single-layer system in which the layer is compatible with an existing video format - HDR10 (8.1), HLG (8.4) or SDR (8.2)." Wikipedia: Profile 8 single-layer, no enhancement layer; Profile 7 explicitly the dual-layer (BL+EL→12-bit) profile.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace 'both dual-layer-compatible streaming profiles' with 'both single-layer streaming profiles — the base layer is backward-compatible (HDR10 / HLG) and the DV RPU adds dynamic metadata; contrast with the dual-layer Profile 7 used on UHD Blu-ray.' (The entry only covers 8.1/8.4, so the SDR/8.2 reference in the original evidence is contextual, not part of the on-page text to correct.)
findings.verifierNote: Read line 2547 verbatim; both Dolby support ("Profile 8 is a single-layer system") and Wikipedia confirm 8.x is single-layer with Profile 7 being the dual-layer one. The 'dual-layer-compatible' label is a genuine inversion — confirmed.

findings.title: EBU R128 tolerance figure attributed to the wrong programme type (live vs non-live swapped)
findings.location: src/lib/glossary-data.json:2815 (term "EBU R128", line 2807)
findings.problem: The definition reads "integrated programme loudness targets -23 LUFS (tolerance about ±0.5 LU for live)". This is backwards. Under the EBU R128-derived delivery specs (e.g. UK DPP), the ±0.5 LU tolerance applies to NON-LIVE (file-based) programmes; LIVE programmes get the looser ±1.0 LU tolerance because they cannot be re-normalised in post. As written it names the wrong programme type and implies live is held to the tighter figure, which a dub-stage mixer or QC operator would dispute.
findings.evidence: Verified at line 2815: text reads "(tolerance about ±0.5 LU for live)". DPP Technical Delivery Standards: "Program Loudness to be targeted as -23.0 LUFS, with a metering tolerance of ±0.5 LU for non live content, and a tolerance of ±1 LU for live content." Confirms ±0.5 LU = non-live, ±1.0 LU = live — the opposite of the entry. (The entry's max true peak of -1 dBTP is correct.)
findings.suggestedFix: Change to: "...targets -23 LUFS (tolerance ±0.5 LU for non-live/file-based, ±1.0 LU for live) with a maximum true peak of -1 dBTP."
findings.verifierNote: Read line 2815 verbatim; DPP delivery standard explicitly assigns ±0.5 LU to non-live and ±1 LU to live — entry's "±0.5 LU for live" is reversed. Confirmed.
findings.domain: Glossary accuracy

findings.title: EBU colour bars: 75% nomenclature given but 100% (8-bar) layout described — internally inconsistent
findings.location: src/lib/glossary-data.json:2786 (term "EBU colour bars", line 2778)
findings.problem: The definition says "The European 100/0/75/0 vertical colour-bar test pattern (eight full-height bars)". These two facts contradict each other. The 100/0/75/0 designation (white 100%, colour bars 75%) corresponds to the SEVEN-colour-bar pattern. The eight full-field equal-width bar pattern is 100/0/100/0 (100% saturation, ITU-R BT.1729). The entry pairs the 75% number with the 8-bar (100%) description, so one of them is wrong. A colourist or QC engineer treats 75% vs 100% bars as materially different references.
findings.evidence: Verified at line 2786: text reads "100/0/75/0 vertical colour-bar test pattern (eight full-height bars)". ITU-R BT.1729 / Wikipedia EBU colour bars: 100/0/100/0 = eight vertical bars of 100% intensity (full-field); 100/0/75/0 = seven bars at 75% saturation. The eight-bar full-field pattern is the 100% (100/0/100/0) version, not the 75% one. Internal contradiction confirmed.
findings.suggestedFix: Make it consistent: for the eight-bar full-field pattern use "100/0/100/0 ... (eight full-height bars)"; or if the 75% pattern is intended use "100/0/75/0 ... (seven colour bars plus a 100% white bar)." (Minor: the four figures are white level / black level / colour-bar intensity / black-setup level; the finding's 'MaxColourBars/MinColourBars' gloss is close enough and does not affect the contradiction.)
findings.verifierNote: Read line 2786 verbatim; ITU-R BT.1729 and Wikipedia confirm 100/0/100/0 = 8 bars (100%) and 100/0/75/0 = 7 bars (75%). The entry pairs the 75% code with an 8-bar description — genuine internal inconsistency. Confirmed.

findings.title: "Audio pull" % is inverted — contradicts the +pitch it shows and the industry pull-up convention
findings.location: src/lib/postcalc.ts:88 (conform: audioPullPct = (src.actual / tgt.actual) * 100); displayed in src/components/Tools.tsx:166
findings.problem: For a 24→25 PAL conform the picture plays FASTER, so the audio must be sped UP to stay in sync — the industry calls this a "4% pull-up" (factor 25/24 = 104.167%). The tool instead computes audioPullPct = src/tgt = 24/25 = 96.000% and labels it simply "Audio pull". 96% reads to any sound engineer as a slow-DOWN to 96% speed (a pull-DOWN). This directly contradicts the two things shown right next to it: the "Pitch shift" stat (+0.706 st, i.e. UP) and the panel's own footnote "audio pulled up ~+0.71 st". I verified the conflict across all four NTSC/PAL cases: whenever picture plays faster, semitones is positive (up) but audioPullPct is <100 (reads as slower) — pull-vs-pitch always disagree. A colourist/sound mixer setting a Pro Tools SRC pull for 24→25 enters +4.0% / 104.167%, never 96%.
findings.suggestedFix: Make the audio-pull figure consistent with the pitch/speed direction the tool already shows. Either compute audioPullPct = (tgt.actual / src.actual) * 100 (so 24→25 reads 104.167%, a pull-up, matching +0.71 st), or relabel the existing 96% value explicitly as "audio length vs original" / "resample to" so it can't be read as a speed/pull factor. The pull % and the semitone sign must point the same way.
findings.verifierNote: Ran conform(86400,24,25): speedPct=96.000% (UI labels <100 'faster'), audioPullPct=96.000%, semitones=+0.7067. 12*log2(104.167/100)=+0.7067 vs 12*log2(96/100)=-0.7067 — pull factor and shown pitch derive from opposite ratios. All 4 NTSC/PAL cases: pull<100 always disagrees with semitone-up. Web (wiki.robotz.com PAL_Speedup) confirms 24→25 = 4% speed-up, playback factor 25/24=104.16%, audio pulled UP.

findings.title: Docs claim "audio-only tracks are skipped" but the EDL path keeps every audio event
findings.location: src/lib/edl.ts:26-55 (parseEDL) and the header comment in src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:7-8 ("audio-only tracks are skipped"), also surfaced in Tools.tsx:334
findings.problem: parseEDL pushes every event line regardless of the TRACK field — V, A, A2, AA are all retained. So for a CMX3600 EDL containing audio events (extremely common in conformed/online EDLs), the converter outputs them all, despite the file-level comment and the UI caption "transitions become cuts, audio-only tracks skipped". The audio-skip behaviour exists only incidentally on the XML paths (which query <video> clipitems and hardcode track:'V'); the EDL — the format the tool is named after and ships a Sample for — does no track filtering at all. A picture-cut-list user gets phantom audio rows mixed into their video cut list.
findings.suggestedFix: Either actually filter the EDL events (e.g. drop events whose track matches /^A/i unless the user opts in) to honour the stated cut-list contract, or remove/soften the "audio-only tracks skipped" claim from the comment and the Tools.tsx caption so it doesn't promise behaviour the EDL path doesn't deliver.
findings.verifierNote: Parsed a mixed EDL (V+A+A2+AA): result tracks=[V,A,A2,AA], 4 events, zero filtering. Comment 'audio-only tracks are skipped' present at sequenceConvert.ts:7-8; UI caption at Tools.tsx:334. The XML path at line 63 queries 'media > video clipitem' so it does drop audio there, but EDL/CSV do not — finding's framing is accurate.

findings.title: FCPXML source in/out double-counts the asset's start time (off by the source TC, often ~1h)
findings.location: src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:124 (const srcBase = (asset?.start || 0) + start;) used at lines 130-131
findings.problem: In FCPXML the asset-clip's `start` attribute is already expressed in the asset's own timeline — the same coordinate space whose origin is the asset's `start`. The correct source in-point is the clip's `start` directly. This code adds asset.start + clip.start, double-counting the asset origin. For any media with an embedded source timecode (i.e. asset start != "0s" — typical for camera/broadcast clips whose start is e.g. 3600s = 01:00:00:00), every Src In/Src Out is offset high by the asset start (often a full hour or more). Files where asset.start = 0s happen to come out right, which masks the bug on toy exports.
findings.suggestedFix: Use srcBase = start (the asset-clip's start) directly, and do not add asset.start. If you want to support clips that reference media without their own start, fall back to asset.start only when the element has no start attribute. Keep the "experimental" label, but the default math should match the spec.
findings.verifierNote: Traced srcBase=(asset.start||0)+start: asset.start=3600,clip.start=3601 → 7201 (~02:00:01) vs correct 3601; asset.start=0 case returns clip value unchanged (masks bug). Apple FCPXML ref: 'start' defines a local timeline origin; fcp.cafe example shows asset.start and asset-clip.start carrying the IDENTICAL value (651158508/180000s) i.e. one coordinate space — adding doubles it. Stays medium: FCPXML path is explicitly labelled experimental.

findings.title: Drop-frame parser silently accepts non-existent DF timecodes (e.g. 00:01:00;00) and maps them to the wrong frame
findings.location: src/lib/postcalc.ts:31-42 (tcToFrames) / 22-28 (parseTC)
findings.problem: In 29.97/59.94 DF the labels :00 and :01 at the top of every non-tenth minute do not exist (they are the dropped counts). A correct DF parser rejects them. tcToFrames accepts 00:01:00;00 and returns 1798, and 00:01:00;01 → 1799 — but framesToTC(1798) = 00:00:59;28 and framesToTC(1799) = 00:00:59;29, so these inputs are non-injective: they round-trip to a *different* label than typed. A user pasting/adding an illegal DF value gets a silently shifted frame count with no warning, which can throw an EDL conform off by up to 2 frames per affected minute boundary.
findings.suggestedFix: In tcToFrames, when df is true reject frames f<dropFrames when m%10!==0 and s===0 (return null), so illegal DF labels surface as "invalid" rather than mapping to an adjacent frame. (Tolerating them is a defensible convenience, but then the value should at least be normalised/round-tripped rather than left non-injective.)
findings.verifierNote: tcToFrames('00:01:00;00',30,DF)=1798→framesToTC=00:00:59;28 (NON-injective); '00:01:00;01'=1799→00:00:59;29; '00:01:00;02'=1800→00:01:00;02 (injective). Legal tenth-minute '00:10:00;00'=17982 round-trips correctly. parseTC only checks mm<=59,s<=59,f<nominal — no DF-legality check. Suggested guard (m%10!==0 && s===0 && f<dropFrames) is the right condition.

findings.title: EDL writer emits real transitions (D nnn / Wxxx) although the converter only ever produces single-line cuts — malformed dissolve syntax
findings.location: src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:189-199 (toEDL) — `const trans = (e.transition || "C") + (e.transDur ? "" : "");` and the transDur padding
findings.problem: Two issues. (1) The line `const trans = (e.transition||'C') + (e.transDur ? '' : '')` is dead: both branches append '' — a no-op that was presumably meant to suppress something. (2) More importantly, when an event carries transition='D' with a transDur (as the bundled Sample EDL does: event 002 'D 025'), toEDL writes a single dissolve line `002 ... D 025 ...`. A valid CMX3600 dissolve requires TWO synchronised lines (a cut to the outgoing 'from', then the 'D nnn' to the incoming 'to'); a lone 'D' line has no 'from' source and many onlining systems reject or mis-resolve it. The tool's stated contract is "transitions become cuts", but the writer faithfully re-emits the transition opcode instead of degrading it to 'C', so the output is neither a clean cut list nor a valid dissolve.
findings.suggestedFix: Decide on one behaviour and make the writer match the doc: to honour "transitions become cuts", force the written transition to 'C' and drop transDur on EDL output; or, to preserve dissolves, emit the proper two-line CMX3600 dissolve. Also delete the no-op `+ (e.transDur ? '' : '')`.
findings.verifierNote: Ran toEDL on the parsed Sample EDL: event 2 emitted as single line '002  AX       V    D    025 ...' — no paired cut line, contradicting the 'transitions become cuts' caption. Confirmed the ternary (e.transition||'C')+(e.transDur?'':'') evaluates identically on both branches ('D' either way) — a genuine no-op.

findings.title: xmeml in/out are treated as inclusive but FCP7/Premiere XML out-points are exclusive — Src/Rec Out is one frame long
findings.location: src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:79-82 (srcOut uses fileTc+out, recOut uses seqTc+end)
findings.problem: In FCP7/Premiere xmeml the clipitem <out> and <end> values are exclusive (one frame past the last used frame), matching the half-open convention. EDL Src Out/Rec Out, by contrast, are conventionally the record-out as written by the NLE and tools differ, but if you map xmeml <end> straight into an EDL out-point you get a duration that is one frame longer than the clip actually is for every event (or, depending on the downstream tool's own convention, a consistent +1 frame offset). For a post supervisor reconciling durations against the original timeline this 1-frame-per-event discrepancy is exactly the kind of error that fails a conform QC.
findings.suggestedFix: Decide and document the out-point convention. If targeting EDL/cut-list durations, subtract 1 frame from the xmeml-derived out/end (or clearly state outputs use exclusive out-points) so durations match the source timeline. Apply the same rule consistently to FCPXML (where duration is already a count, so srcOut = srcBase+duration is one-past-last and may also warrant -1 for display).
findings.verifierNote: Apple FCP7 XML Interchange Format spec confirms: out/end use 1-based numbering (first frame = 1) while in/start use 0-based — i.e. out/end are exclusive/one-past-last. Code at lines 79-82 applies fileTc+out and seqTc+end verbatim with no -1. Real concern; kept medium because the 'correct' EDL out-point convention itself varies by downstream tool, as the finding acknowledges.

findings.title: EDL/FCM drop-frame flag is treated as one value for the whole sequence; per-event FCM switches are lost
findings.location: src/lib/edl.ts:36 & 54 (only last FCM kept) and src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:185 (single df for the whole ParseResult)
findings.problem: CMX3600 allows an FCM: line to appear between events to switch drop-frame mode mid-list (mixed-rate or mixed-DF reels in one EDL). parseEDL stores only the last FCM into a single `fcm` field, and parseSequence derives one df flag applied to the entire sequence. A mixed EDL is therefore re-timecoded under a single DF assumption, and on re-export every event gets one FCM header. A working editor handing over a real-world onlined EDL can get silently mislabeled timecode.
findings.suggestedFix: Track FCM per event (carry the current FCM state onto each EdlEvent as it is parsed) and honour it in framesToTC/round-trips; on export, emit an FCM line only when the mode changes. At minimum, warn the user when more than one FCM appears.
findings.verifierNote: Parsed an EDL with FCM switching mid-list (NON-DROP → DROP → NON-DROP): final scalar fcm='NON-DROP FRAME' (only last kept); EdlEvent objects carry no per-event FCM field. parseSequence:185 derives one df via /DROP/i.test(fcm). No per-event FCM tracking exists — confirmed.

findings.title: EDL writer can emit reel names >8 chars (default ON), breaking strict CMX3600 ingest
findings.location: src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:192-195 (toEDL, when opts.reelLong) and the default reelLong=true in src/components/Tools.tsx:249 / checkbox label "reel > 8 chars" at line 318
findings.problem: True CMX3600 reels are limited to 8 characters in a fixed column. The converter defaults the "reel > 8 chars" option to ON, so a 16-char reel is written unpadded, shifting every subsequent column on that line. Many hardware/online ingesters and some software parse EDL by column position and will reject or misread these lines. Defaulting to the non-standard long form (rather than the spec-compliant 8-char truncation) is a footgun for the pro user who exports an EDL expecting it to load anywhere.
findings.suggestedFix: Default reelLong to false (spec-compliant 8-char reels), and keep the long-reel option as an explicit opt-in for tools that accept it (Resolve/extended EDL). Optionally warn that long reels are non-standard CMX3600.
findings.verifierNote: toEDL with reelLong=true writes '001  VERYLONGREELNAME V    C ...' (16-char reel inline, pushing track/trans/TC columns right); reelLong=false truncates to 'VERYLONG' (8). useState default at Tools.tsx:249 is reelLong=true. CMX3600 reel field is 8 chars — confirmed.

findings.title: 1.33 is mislabeled "Academy" — Academy aperture is 1.375, not 1.33
findings.location: src/lib/postcalc.ts:97 ({ r: 1.33, name: "4:3 (Academy TV)" })
findings.problem: The named-ratio table calls 1.33 "4:3 (Academy TV)". 1.33 is 4:3 (SDTV / silent-era full aperture), but the Academy ratio is 1.375 (the post-1932 sound aperture, 22:16). Conflating the two under the word "Academy" is a small but real factual slip that a film-literate user (the target audience of this tool) will notice; 1.375 isn't in the table at all.
findings.suggestedFix: Rename the 1.33 entry to "4:3 (1.33 / Full Aperture)" and, if desired, add a separate { r: 1.375, name: "Academy (1.375)" } entry. Reserve the word "Academy" for 1.375.
findings.verifierNote: Source line 97 confirmed: {r:1.33, name:'4:3 (Academy TV)'}; no 1.375 entry in NAMED_RATIOS. Web (Wikipedia/Grokipedia Academy ratio): Academy = 1.375:1, standardized by SMPE 1932 sound aperture; 1.33:1 = pre-1930s silent full-aperture / 4:3 SDTV. Genuinely distinct ratios — label is a real factual slip.

findings.title: Pasted CSV whose header lacks reel/clip/src keywords is misdetected as EDL → "0 events" with no helpful reason
findings.location: src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:28-38 (detectFormat) — the CSV heuristic at line 36 requires reel|clip|src in the header, else falls through to return "edl"
findings.problem: detectFormat only classifies extension-less pasted text as CSV when the first line contains a comma AND one of reel/clip/src. A perfectly valid comma-separated cut list with differently-named headers (or an export with a leading index/notes column) falls through to "edl", parseEDL finds nothing, and the user sees "No events found" with the format mislabeled as CMX3600 EDL — no hint that it was parsed as the wrong format. For a converter this is the most common real-world failure mode.
findings.suggestedFix: Broaden the CSV fallback: if the file has no XML/EDL signature but the first non-empty line contains commas and multiple subsequent rows have a consistent comma count, treat it as CSV. Surface the detected format prominently and, on 0 events, suggest "try a different format" rather than failing silently as EDL.
findings.verifierNote: detectFormat('a,b,c\n1,2,3')='edl'; even a real-world header 'Event #,Tape,Name In,Name Out\n...' → 'edl' (no reel/clip/src keyword). Only headers containing reel|clip|src route to csv (verified 'event,reel,clip'→'csv'). Falls through to return 'edl' at line 37 — confirmed.

findings.title: PDF column truncation assumes 4.6pt/char but Courier-8pt advance is 4.8pt — wide cells can collide
findings.location: src/lib/sequenceConvert.ts:221 (const max = Math.floor(c.w / 4.6)) in toPDF
findings.problem: Courier at 8pt has a fixed advance of 0.6em = 4.8pt per character. The truncation budget uses 4.6, which is optimistic: a cell filled to `max` chars is ~4% wider than its column and can overrun into the neighbour. Timecode cells (11 chars) are safe, but a long clip name truncated to exactly max can still kiss the next column. Minor cosmetic risk in the PDF cut list.
findings.suggestedFix: Use 4.8 (or 4.81) as the per-char width for the 8pt Courier truncation, or measure with pdf.getTextWidth() and trim until it fits the column minus a small gutter.
findings.verifierNote: Courier advance = 8pt*0.6 = 4.8pt/char (standard monospace metric); code uses 4.6 at line 221. Per-column check: Clip col w=210 → floor(210/4.6)=45 chars → 45*4.8=216pt = +6pt overflow into Src In. Other columns stay within width (Reel 13ch→62.4<64; TC cells hold only 11ch). Real but cosmetic — enhancement severity correct.

findings.title: Fractional/garbage frame entries are silently rounded/accepted in the Timecode and Frames inputs
findings.location: src/components/Tools.tsx:95 (frInt = Math.round(Number(frames))) and 88-89 defaults; postcalc.ts:47 framesToTC rounds too
findings.problem: The Frames input accepts '12.9' (→13), '1e6', and empty (Number('')=0 → shows 00:00:00:00 rather than blank/invalid). Frame counts are integers by definition; silently rounding a fractional entry or interpreting blank as 0 can mask a typo for a user doing a careful conform. Low impact but unprofessional for a precision tool.
findings.suggestedFix: Reject non-integer/empty frame input (show 'invalid' unless the string matches /^\d+$/), and treat empty as no-result rather than 0.
findings.verifierNote: Math.round(Number('12.9'))=13; Math.round(Number('1e6'))=1000000 (both accepted); Number('')=0 is finite so frTc renders 00:00:00:00 not '—'. /^\d+$/ rejects both '12.9' and '1e6', matching the suggested guard. Confirmed at Tools.tsx:95-96 and framesToTC rounds at postcalc.ts:47.
